branchingScenario.title: AN68.1-3 | Nervous tissue histology — Clinical Case

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 35-year-old man presents with ascending weakness and areflexia following a viral infection. Work through this case applying your knowledge of neuron structure, myelination, and peripheral nerve organisation.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified the histological basis of Guillain-Barre syndrome and demonstrated strong knowledge of nervous tissue organisation.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review Schwann cells vs oligodendrocytes and the connective tissue sheaths of peripheral nerves.
Revisit neuron ultrastructure, myelinating cells in PNS and CNS, and the epineurium-perineurium-endoneurium hierarchy.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 35-year-old man presents with progressive ascending weakness in both legs over 5 days, preceded by a diarrhoeal illness 2 weeks ago. Deep tendon reflexes are absent. Nerve conduction studies show prolonged distal motor latencies — suggesting demyelination of peripheral nerves.

This is Guillain-Barré syndrome — an autoimmune demyelinating polyneuropathy. In the PNS, myelin is produced by Schwann cells, which are the targets of the autoimmune attack.

You recall that multipolar neurons have a large polygonal soma (cell body) with abundant Nissl bodies (rough endoplasmic reticulum) in the cytoplasm.

branchingQuestion.question: In Guillain-Barré syndrome, the autoimmune attack targets myelin in peripheral nerves. Which cells produce myelin in the PNS versus the CNS?

alternatives.text: Schwann cells in the PNS; oligodendrocytes in the CNS
Oligodendrocytes in the PNS; astrocytes in the CNS

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: It's the reverse — Schwann cells myelinate in the PNS, and oligodendrocytes myelinate in the CNS. Astrocytes provide structural support but do not produce myelin.

alternatives.text: Microglia in the PNS; Schwann cells in the CNS

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Microglia are immune cells of the CNS. Schwann cells produce myelin in the PNS, while oligodendrocytes produce myelin in the CNS.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Schwann cells (PNS) and oligodendrocytes (CNS) are the myelinating cells.

A nerve biopsy is considered. You recall the connective tissue organisation of a peripheral nerve:

  • Epineurium: The outermost sheath surrounding the entire nerve
  • Perineurium: Wraps each fascicle within the nerve
  • Endoneurium: Surrounds individual nerve fibres

The biopsy confirms segmental demyelination with inflammatory infiltrates, consistent with Guillain-Barré syndrome.

branchingQuestion.question: On the nerve biopsy, you identify the connective tissue layers. Which layer surrounds each fascicle within the peripheral nerve?

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Epineurium is the outermost sheath surrounding the entire nerve. Perineurium is the layer that wraps each individual fascicle.

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Endoneurium surrounds individual nerve fibres within a fascicle. The layer wrapping each fascicle is the perineurium.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key points from this case:

  • Neuron structure: Multipolar neurons have a polygonal soma with Nissl bodies (RER)
  • PNS myelin: Schwann cells; CNS myelin: Oligodendrocytes
  • Nerve sheaths: Epineurium (whole nerve) → perineurium (fascicle) → endoneurium (individual fibre)
  • Guillain-Barré: Autoimmune demyelination of peripheral nerves → prolonged distal motor latencies
